main_summary.summary: Robert L. Carter, born 1917 in Caryville, Florida, recounts his journey from Newark and East Orange childhood through Lincoln University (where he debated and agitated for black faculty), Howard University School of Law (entered 1937 on scholarship under dean William H. Hastie), and Columbia, where his Rosenwald Fellowship thesis under Noel Dowling addressed First Amendment strike rights. Carter describes attending Marian Anderson's 1939 Lincoln Memorial concert after being refused at Constitution Hall by the DAR, his Army Air Corps service (which radicalized him against segregation through encounters with a Lieutenant Colonel and the officers' club), and his recruitment by Hastie to Thurgood Marshall's NAACP staff in 1944. He argued his first Supreme Court case in McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents, worked alongside Marshall, William T. Coleman Jr., Spottswood Robinson, and Constance Baker Motley, and recruited Kenneth and Mamie Clark, after Otto Klineberg's referral, whose doll test grounded the Clarendon County (Briggs) case and the Brown v. Board victory. Carter became NAACP General Counsel in 1955, defended the organization in NAACP v. Alabama and NAACP v. Button against southern attempts to outlaw it, attacked northern school segregation through Ocean Hill-Brownsville and Intermediate School 201, and resigned in 1968 over the firing of Lewis M. Steel. He later became a federal judge.
key_themes: social science evidence in constitutional litigation
Howard Law School and Charles Hamilton Houston's program
NAACP defense against southern outlawing campaigns
main_summary.historical_significance: Carter is the primary surviving architect of the NAACP Legal Defense Fund litigation strategy that produced Brown v. Board of Education. His testimony documents the intellectual lineage from Charles Hamilton Houston's separate-but-equal exhaustion strategy through the McLaurin and Sweatt precedents to the per-se-unconstitutional argument that prevailed in Brown, and his account of recruiting Kenneth and Mamie Clark provides first-person attribution of the doll-test evidence that grounded Chief Justice Warren's unanimous opinion. The interview is also a primary source on the NAACP's defense of its own organizational existence after Brown (NAACP v. Alabama, NAACP v. Button), Carter's pivot to northern de facto segregation cases including Ocean Hill-Brownsville and Intermediate School 201, his 1968 resignation over the firing of attorney Lewis M. Steel, and the internal post-Marshall power struggle with Jack Greenberg at the LDF.

chapters.part: A Child of the Great Migration
chapters.title: Not Named for Robert E. Lee
chapters.summary: Robert L. Carter opens by saying his name, Robert Lee Carter, born in Caryville, Florida, and reflects on whether he was named after Robert E. Lee, since Southern generals were considered heroic by some Black people of that period.

chapters.part: A Child of the Great Migration
chapters.title: Free Sixty Years After the War
chapters.summary: Carter describes being born in 1917 in Caryville, his family of free Black people who left the deep South in the Great Migration about sixty years after the Civil War, and his father, a migrant agricultural worker sent north to work in a factory in Newark.

chapters.part: A Child of the Great Migration
chapters.title: Washing Clothes for White People
chapters.summary: Carter recounts his father dying when he was a year old, his mother having too little schooling for any work but washing white people's clothes, the family living in poverty until he was twelve, and his older sister marrying a man who owned a bar in Linden, New Jersey, which lifted the family's living conditions.

chapters.part: A Child of the Great Migration
chapters.title: A Bathroom of My Own
chapters.summary: He describes his sister's marriage opening the door to mainstream prerogatives, having an indoor toilet for the first time after years of outhouses, and his reflections on how the poor live their lives on the street while middle-class people do the same behind doors.

chapters.part: A Child of the Great Migration
chapters.title: The Library Close By
chapters.summary: Carter explains that, not skillful with his hands, he saw a good education as his only way into the mainstream, was considered smart and skipped grades, and spent much of his time reading novels and history in a nearby public library, though he calls his reading undisciplined.

chapters.part: A Child of the Great Migration
chapters.title: The Gods After My Sisters
chapters.summary: He recalls the segregated poverty of Newark, his sisters at age sixteen amid the migration from the South, and his mother's desperation to get the family out, fearing the men on the street would get her daughters pregnant, a worry she did not extend to him.

chapters.title: Stay With Your Own Kind
chapters.summary: Carter remembers the sixth grade, a white girl and a Black girl who were close friends, and a teacher who told the white girl to stay with her own kind, and contrasts Newark teachers who let things play out with East Orange teachers who actively intervened to impose segregation.

chapters.title: The Pool Cleaned After Us
chapters.summary: He describes the gym-class swimming pool that Black students could use only on separate days before it was drained and cleaned, and how, learning of a Supreme Court ruling that school facilities had to be open to all, he decided he would use the pool.

chapters.title: They Closed It Rather Than Open It
chapters.summary: Carter recounts going to the pool alone every gym day though he could not swim and was terrified of the water, being threatened by teachers, and the school responding not by opening the pool to everyone but by closing it entirely, costing it a championship swim team and teaching him the price whites paid for segregation.

chapters.title: A Good Test Taker at Lincoln
chapters.summary: He tells of enrolling at Lincoln University at sixteen, being considered brilliant and a very good test taker, and reflects that learning to take tests is a gift that does not necessarily mean intellectual gifts, while calling Lincoln a wonderful, isolated experience.

chapters.title: A Predominantly Black Campus
chapters.summary: Carter describes Lincoln as one of the great times of his life for the friends he made on an isolated, predominantly Black campus, an all-male school whose professors were retired from Princeton, and the rowdy student behavior that earlier generations had been allowed to indulge in.

chapters.title: Debating the Small White Colleges
chapters.summary: He recalls his generation agitating for Black faculty when only one professor was Black, and joining the debating team that competed against small white colleges like Haverford, which gave him the courage and understanding that he could compete with white people.

chapters.title: You Did Not Need a Big Voice
chapters.summary: Carter explains going to Howard University School of Law on scholarship, his early fear that a good lawyer needed a big voice, and learning from his idol, Dean William Hastie, that what mattered was knowing all the facts and being conversant with the law rather than oratory.

chapters.title: A Program to Fight Segregation
chapters.summary: He describes Charles Hamilton Houston's program to fight the separate-but-equal doctrine of Plessy v. Ferguson by forcing states to provide duplicate courses for Black students, leading graduate schools to begin accepting Black students, and his own first Supreme Court case, McLaurin, where a student was made to sit at a separate seat and table.

chapters.title: The Day Before the Argument
chapters.topic: Watching Houston in the Gaines Case
chapters.summary: Carter recalls Houston, while Carter was a student, trying the Gaines case, Missouri ex rel. Gaines, in which a Black applicant sought equal educational opportunity where the state had no law school for Black students, and notes a tradition of arguing each case at the law school the day before the Supreme Court.
related_events: Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada

chapters.title: A Tradition of Moot Court
chapters.summary: He explains the tradition by which every Supreme Court argument brought under NAACP and Legal Defense Fund auspices was first argued at Howard's law school the day before, a justice once echoing a question a student had posed, and reflects on his own nervousness watching Houston argue.

chapters.title: He Turned His Back on Houston
chapters.summary: Carter recounts the story that Justice James Clark McReynolds turned his back throughout Houston's argument in the Gaines case, and the barber's tale that a Black barber told McReynolds that Houston was qualified to sit on the Supreme Court and would represent all the people, and remembers Houston as a formidable man who left the NAACP over money worries while Hastie remained his icon.

chapters.title: I Just Went to the Concert
chapters.summary: He tells how, working as a busboy and waiter to support his Howard scholarship, he was refused the day off for Marian Anderson's Lincoln Memorial concert, arranged by Mrs. Roosevelt after the Daughters of the American Revolution barred her from Constitution Hall, and went anyway, weeping at the historic performance.

chapters.title: Fired and Then a Full Scholarship
chapters.summary: Carter recalls returning to a chaotic dining room and being fired as expected, then receiving a full scholarship to work part-time in the library, and describes the integrated crowd in tears at the Lincoln Memorial, where Marian Anderson, the first Black woman to sing there, seemed to be making history.

chapters.title: A First Amendment Thesis
chapters.summary: He describes leaving Howard for Columbia on a Rosenwald Fellowship, working toward a doctorate on a thesis that the First Amendment right to strike was essential to the democratic process, and weighing whether to write part of his dissertation or take a full load of courses for his master's.

chapters.title: The Court Follows the Vote
chapters.summary: Carter recounts choosing the technician Noel Dowling as his advisor, a man who thought Supreme Court differences turned on small legal changes rather than the social scene, and his own belief that the Court's changes followed the vote and reflected political change, while learning that arguing fine distinctions let the Court rule without admitting it was being political.

chapters.part: The Army Made a Militant of Me
chapters.title: I Did Not Resist the Draft
chapters.summary: Carter explains that, feeling he had exhausted the avenues of fighting segregation and worried about his career, he did not resist the draft in the summer of 1941, and was already in the Army when Pearl Harbor brought the United States into World War II.

chapters.part: The Army Made a Militant of Me
chapters.title: Clearing Bush in Augusta
chapters.topic: Black Recruits and a Staff Sergeant
chapters.summary: He describes the NAACP's fight for Black men to enter the Army as more than servants, the long-serving Black staff sergeants who were the only ones with standing, and his own assignment clearing trees in Augusta, Georgia, in the Army Air Corps under an ignorant sergeant.

chapters.part: The Army Made a Militant of Me
chapters.title: His Personal Lawyer
chapters.summary: Carter recalls becoming the legal advisor to the staff sergeant in charge of him, handling the man's domestic problems to get out of the work detail, and frames the Army experience, with its devastating racism, as what set him on the course of a civil rights lawyer.

chapters.part: The Army Made a Militant of Me
chapters.title: Officer Candidate School
chapters.summary: He describes the agitation that opened officer candidate school to Black recruits, his becoming a member of the quartermaster corps in the still-segregated Army, and reacting to his own written line that the Army experience made a militant of him, since enlisted whites had no respect for Black officers.

chapters.part: The Army Made a Militant of Me
chapters.title: Accused of Inciting a Riot
chapters.summary: Carter recounts becoming an officer, a second lieutenant denied respect, bringing actions against several who would not treat him properly, and being accused by the base officer of trying to incite a riot by himself, while refusing the offer to live off the base.

chapters.part: The Army Made a Militant of Me
chapters.title: When You Learn Some Manners
chapters.topic: The Lieutenant Colonel and the Hanger
chapters.summary: He tells of a lieutenant colonel who barged into his quarters looking for a coat hanger, called him a racial slur on seeing he was Black, invoked his many grades of seniority, and was told by Carter that until he learned some manners and apologized he would get no respect, and to get the hell out of his room.

chapters.part: The Army Made a Militant of Me
chapters.title: I Was Not Trying to Revolutionize Things
chapters.summary: Carter recounts bringing charges against a couple of white officers who showed him no respect, the base commander pressing him over the trouble, his insistence that as an officer he was entitled to respect or he would take the fight to Washington, and his prompt transfer.

chapters.part: The Army Made a Militant of Me
chapters.title: The Bartender Looked as Frightened as I Felt
chapters.topic: A Drink at the Officers' Club
chapters.summary: He describes going to the bar at the officers' club where a Black bartender, looking as frightened as Carter felt, hesitated to serve him, finally poured the drink, could not take his cash, and a white officer put it on his bill, an experience so devastating that Carter never tried it again.

chapters.part: The Army Made a Militant of Me
chapters.title: Vicious and Virulent
chapters.summary: Carter reflects that before the Army his friendships had led him to think discrimination was not really an issue and that hard work could beat the system, until the vicious, virulent hatred against Black soldiers opened his eyes that segregation had to be fought and that education did not spare a person from it.

chapters.part: The Army Made a Militant of Me
chapters.title: The Army That Changed the Most
chapters.summary: He recalls coming out of the Army determined to devote his life to fighting segregation, and marvels at the irony that the Army and Air Force became the institutions in the country most accepting of diversity and equal opportunity.

chapters.title: I Had Never Heard of Thurgood Marshall
chapters.topic: A Country Bumpkin From East Orange
chapters.summary: Carter describes coming out of the Army in 1944 as a country bumpkin from East Orange who had never heard of Thurgood Marshall, William Hastie, Roy Wilkins, or the NAACP tradition, having gotten into trouble in the Army for refusing to accept segregation.

chapters.title: Taken On to Be a Snitch
chapters.summary: He recounts the Army taking him on as a suspected snitch meant to report troublemakers, the officers' amusement turning to labeling him a troublemaker himself when they found he took a liberal newspaper, and his reconnecting after discharge with William Hastie, then dean of Howard Law, who with Marshall recruited him.

chapters.title: Drafted Again
chapters.topic: Settling With the Army and Joining the Staff
chapters.summary: Carter tells of being drafted again, his trouble with the Army, following Hastie's advice to seek to rejoin in some capacity only to be turned down by a general, his reflection that he could have been a better soldier with some freedom of action, and his resolve to spend his life fighting segregation at the NAACP.

chapters.title: Cadres to Their Hometowns
chapters.summary: He describes the NAACP program at Howard designing cases to desegregate state institutions, training cadres of Black lawyers to return to their hometowns and bring litigation, and Houston's strategy of forcing states either to set up duplicate programs and accept Black students or face suits over separate-but-equal facilities.

chapters.title: A Couple of Rooms to Myself
chapters.summary: Carter recalls 1944 and his first contact with Thurgood Marshall, being given a couple of small offices because of his education while the rest of the staff worked in a big room, and a moment when the staff had to prepare a briefing on a separate-but-equal case while Marshall was out at lunch.

chapters.title: The Brief Was Done When He Got Back
chapters.summary: He tells how, reluctant but told by Marshall's secretary to do the briefing during the long lunch, he had the brief done by the time Marshall returned, who looked it over and sent it out, and from then on Carter became the one writing the briefs behind the scenes, long without credit.

chapters.title: The First Black Woman Lawyer They Had Seen
chapters.summary: Carter describes traveling the South to try cases, the teacher-salary case with Constance Baker Motley, the first Black woman lawyer many in Mississippi had seen, and the people's ecstatic reaction to watching them put a white superintendent on the hot seat under cross-examination.

chapters.title: Reenacted in the Barbershops
chapters.summary: He recounts how, because federal courtrooms were not segregated, Black spectators packed them to watch, vicariously living through the spectacle of white superintendents made to answer questions, and reenacting the courtroom drama afterward in barbershops and beauty parlors across town.

chapters.title: Hotels for Black Friends
chapters.summary: Carter reflects on the courtroom theater fading over time, and describes traveling the segregated South as a young lawyer with poor or no hotel accommodations, arguing the figure case among the first of the Brown cases with Jack Greenberg, and eventually staying with Black friends to find decent lodging.

chapters.title: A Rope Around Him
chapters.summary: He describes McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents, his first Supreme Court case, in which McLaurin had the same books and professors but was made to sit in a special seat walled off from his classmates, and the argument that segregation denied him the intellectual intercourse essential to equal education.

chapters.title: A Good Publicist for the Cause
chapters.summary: Carter recounts the doubt among supporters that the McLaurin differences were too insubstantial to win, and how Thurgood Marshall, a shrewd publicist, brought such cases and made public statements to deny segregation's supporters their day, proving correct even as critics predicted defeat in the next case.

chapters.title: Attacking Plessy Directly
chapters.summary: He explains the June 1950 shift to taking only cases attacking segregation per se as unconstitutional rather than concentrating on unequal facilities, since a state could equalize facilities and the side might still win, while keeping unequal facilities as a fallback argument.

chapters.title: Make the Cases Too Expensive
chapters.topic: From Graduate Schools to Grade Schools
chapters.summary: Carter describes Houston's thesis of making segregation cases so expensive that states would admit Black students at the graduate and college level, where the schools were clearly distinct, and the new difficulty at the grade-school level in Brown, where schools were presumably alike, requiring the argument that segregation per se impaired Black children's ability to learn.

chapters.title: The Longer They Stayed, the Higher the Scores
chapters.summary: He recounts preparing the Clarendon County case and learning that Otto Klineberg, a social scientist at Columbia, had a study showing that Black children who migrated from segregated Southern schools to integrated Philadelphia scored higher the longer they stayed in that system, exactly the evidence the lawyers needed.

chapters.title: The Good Doll, the Bad Doll
chapters.topic: Kenneth and Mamie Clark's Doll Test
chapters.summary: Carter tells of Klineberg referring him to Kenneth and Mamie Clark, whose Northside Center for Child Development tested segregation's impact, and their doll test showing children a Black doll and a white doll, the bad doll always identified as Black while the child being interviewed identified with the white one.

chapters.title: Running Around With My Dolls
chapters.topic: The Doll Test in Clarendon County
chapters.summary: He describes the theory that segregation impaired Black children's ability to learn and gave them a sense of inferiority, recalls the Clarendon County case as the only one where the doll test was used in presentation, and the comic moment of Spottswood Robinson watching him run around a South Carolina station with the dolls before understanding their purpose.

chapters.title: Stay Within Your Field
chapters.summary: Carter recounts Kenneth Clark helping recruit a roster of professional expert witnesses, and his own insistence that they testify only to what they could support and never stray outside their field, since the lawyers' job in cross-examination was to become an expert for that moment and punish any witness who overreached.

chapters.title: Make Them Pay for It
chapters.summary: He explains that once the academics relaxed and testified only within their expertise they made good witnesses, while experts on the other side who strayed beyond their level could, with Kenneth Clark's help in evaluation, be made to pay for it under cross-examination.

chapters.title: It Was Not Hard Science
chapters.summary: Carter describes assembling social scientists to sign a brief showing segregation was harmful, the doubts of committee members who distrusted it as not hard science, Marshall allowing him to take the risk because they had nothing else, and William T. Coleman Jr. coming to agree the social science was excellent.

chapters.title: It Had Never Been Argued
chapters.topic: The Argument and Opinion in Brown
chapters.summary: He recounts the consolidated cases coming out of Topeka, Kansas, the argument that equal educational opportunity had never actually been argued in an education case though courts had assumed it, and that the chief justice bought the argument and wrote the opinion overturning separate but equal as unconstitutional.

chapters.title: All Deliberate Speed
chapters.summary: Carter explains expecting the Court to rule unanimously, that the implementation would come with all deliberate speed, and how Topeka, ready to concede and wanting out, led the Court to call the test moot, so it took ten or twenty years before Topeka again reached the point of being willing to end segregation.

chapters.title: Massive Resistance
chapters.summary: He describes all deliberate speed running out only after ten or twenty years of further fighting, the massive resistance across the South after Brown, the absence of meaningful school desegregation until after 1968 and into 1970-71, and reflects on the significance of the decision beyond schools.

chapters.title: Voluntary Segregation Now
chapters.summary: Carter frames Brown's significance as the holding that enforced segregation by law is no longer viable in the country, contrasts it with the voluntary segregation people still accept without a public urge to end it, and expresses hope that a public push will someday make that voluntary segregation no longer viable.

chapters.title: They Went After Us
chapters.summary: He describes the backlash after Brown when Southern states, who had not bothered with the NAACP before, went after it on the theory that outlawing the association would end the problem, and Carter spending a grueling stretch fighting in various states to keep the NAACP alive.

chapters.title: The Right to Seek Opportunity
chapters.topic: NAACP v. Alabama and NAACP v. Button
chapters.summary: Carter recounts arguing that outlawing the association would only push the fight under another name, and the Supreme Court cases NAACP v. Alabama and NAACP v. Button, which upheld the NAACP's right to seek educational opportunity free of state coercion, important for all civil rights organizations.

chapters.title: Reduced to No Counsel
chapters.topic: Becoming General Counsel and the Greenberg Conflict
chapters.summary: He describes becoming General Counsel of the NAACP in 1955 and turning attention to school segregation in the North, and his conflict with Jack Greenberg, who on Thurgood Marshall's departure wanted to run the Legal Defense Fund and reduce Carter to a counsel with no right to bring segregation cases.

chapters.title: I Was Not Going to Turn Them Over
chapters.summary: Carter recounts his bitterness that Greenberg, after Marshall went to the Second Circuit Court of Appeals, tried to get Roy Wilkins to bar Carter from bringing segregation cases and send them all to the Legal Defense Fund, and how, holding all the cases in his own hands, he refused to turn them over and Wilkins would not risk the public fight.

chapters.title: A Black Man Resigning a Good Job
chapters.summary: Carter tells how the NAACP fired staff member Lewis Steel without consulting him over an article, the board of nine men and one Black member meeting the day it appeared, an angry Thurgood Marshall then on the court, and Carter and his whole staff threatening to resign if Steel were not reinstated, a stand he calls a hero's irony for a Black man quitting a good job over a white man's mistreatment.

chapters.title: A New Lease on Life
chapters.topic: Revitalization and the Path to a Judgeship
chapters.summary: Carter describes the resignation giving him a new lease on life when he had felt he had exhausted the avenues of school segregation, probably helping him toward a judgeship, and the earlier Kennedy-era story of Thurgood Marshall being offered only a district seat while Carter was sought instead.

chapters.title: Asked to Pay for the Judgeship
chapters.summary: He recounts Adam Clayton Powell Jr. indicating he had to pay a certain amount of money for a judgeship, telling his advisors which caused an uproar, his people backing down over the politics, and his claim that the fuss they raised got Thurgood Marshall his Court of Appeals appointment while Carter thought his own chance had ended, only to come to pass years later.

chapters.title: Segregation Was Nationwide
chapters.summary: Carter reflects on his pioneering fight in the North through the Ocean Hill-Brownsville and Intermediate School 201 cases, framing segregation as a nationwide phenomenon of keeping Black and white separate rather than a North-South issue, and his hope that a public push will someday confront voluntary school segregation.

chapters.title: We Were All Here That Night
chapters.summary: He discusses the significance of Obama's election, the unexpected passage of one of Obama's education bills, watching the election night, and his own insistence that his goal was never a Black president but equal educational opportunity for all, for the poor and for Black people, as the key to equality.

chapters.title: Younger People Ought to Come After Me
chapters.summary: Carter reflects that his elitist-sounding view of equal education may be right, that he is too old to pursue it and does not want to stay on the stage past his usefulness, trusting younger people to take it over, and admits to feeling mentally fatigued and doing only a little writing now.

chapters.title: His Name Was George
chapters.summary: In a recorded pickup, Carter states that his client George W. McLaurin applied to the University of Oklahoma graduate school and was given only a separate seat in the lunchroom and classroom while taught by the same faculty in the same class, the epitome of separate-but-equal, closing the Library of Congress and Smithsonian presentation.

loc_video.caption: Robert L. Carter oral history interview conducted by Patricia Sullivan in New York, New York, 2010 October 23
